claims.claim_text: GitLab's January 31, 2017 outage involved accidental removal of production database data, and recovery depended on backups and replica state.
claims.rationale: GitLab's official postmortem documents the outage, accidental deletion, and the recovery constraints around backups and replicas.

claims.claim_text: Thirty browser tabs alone can consume 4-6 GB of RAM.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space, pause-and-predict callout
claims.anchor_text: Each program needs counter space, and 30 browser tabs alone can eat 4-6 GB.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Each program needs counter space, and a browser with many tabs open can easily use several gigabytes of RAM.
claims.rationale: Exact tab memory usage varies widely by browser and page content, so this should be presented as an illustrative scenario rather than a fixed number.

claims.claim_text: Windows was the most common desktop operating system worldwide at 60.8% in March 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System: Your Restaurant Manager
claims.rationale: The StatCounter page directly shows Windows at 60.8% worldwide desktop OS share for March 2026.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes supports worker nodes running either Linux or Windows, but the control plane runs on Linux.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System: Your Restaurant Manager, paragraph after OS list
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes documentation explicitly states that the control plane runs on Linux while worker nodes can run either Windows or Linux.

claims.claim_text: AWS listed a Linux t2.large instance with 8 GiB of RAM in us-east-1 at $0.0928/hour on April 15, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Matters for Kubernetes
claims.anchor_text: For example, AWS listed a Linux `t2.large` instance with 8 GiB of RAM in `us-east-1` at `$0.0928/hour` on April 15, 2026.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, a small cloud VM can cost only a few cents per hour, but the exact price depends on the instance type, operating system, region, and purchase model.
claims.rationale: Exact dated cloud prices are unstable snapshots and should not appear as evergreen factual claims.

claims.claim_text: A modern smartphone has more computing power than the Apollo-era computers used for the 1969 moon landing; the Apollo Guidance Computer had 74 KB of memory and a phone has millions of times more.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, phone bullet
claims.anchor_text: A modern smartphone has more computing power than the computers NASA used to land on the moon in 1969. The Apollo Guidance Computer had 74 KB of memory. Your phone has millions of times more.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A modern smartphone is dramatically more capable than the computers used in the Apollo era; for perspective, the Apollo Guidance Computer worked with only tens of kilobytes of memory.
claims.rationale: The exact memory figure is a rounded composite and the multiplier comparison is too precise for evergreen teaching prose.

claims.claim_text: Early computers used magnetic-core memory made of tiny rings, and each ring stored one bit.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, RAM was once magnetic bullet
claims.rationale: The magnetic-core memory reference directly explains that core memory used ring-shaped cores and that each core stored one bit.

claims.claim_text: A full megabyte of core memory would have needed over 8 million tiny rings.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, RAM was once magnetic bullet
claims.anchor_text: A full megabyte would have needed over 8 million tiny rings.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A full megabyte of core memory would have needed millions of tiny rings.
claims.rationale: This is a back-of-the-envelope conversion and is better framed as an approximation than as a precise sourced historical figure.

claims.claim_text: In 1947, operators on the Harvard Mark II found a moth in a relay and logged it as an "actual bug."
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?, first computer bug bullet
claims.rationale: The Harvard Mark II reference directly covers the moth incident, date, and logged note.

claims.claim_text: A team might pay $400/month for a 32GB RAM server when their application only uses 2GB, and right-sizing saves thousands of dollars.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes table, Over-provisioning cloud servers row
claims.anchor_text: A team might pay $400/month for a 32GB RAM server when their application only uses 2GB. Right-sizing saves thousands of dollars.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team might pay hundreds of dollars per month for a larger server than their application actually needs. Right-sizing can save substantial money over time.
claims.rationale: The exact monthly amount and savings vary by provider and workload, but the cost-optimization lesson is well supported.

further_reading.title: Computer
further_reading.why_relevant: A broad beginner-friendly overview of what a computer is and how its major parts fit together.

further_reading.title: Operating system
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful follow-up for the module's explanation of the OS as the part that coordinates hardware and programs.

further_reading.title: Random-access memory
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives additional background on RAM, including why it is temporary and why capacity affects performance.

notes: Excluded analogies, quiz/exercise content, and generic how-to instructions. Focused on dated figures, real incidents, historical anecdotes, and concrete pricing/statistical claims that need support or softening.
_schema_issues: claim[1]:hard_cite_class_cannot_soften:statistic/soften_to_illustration — must be supported, weak_anchor, or needs_allowlist_expansion
claim[5]:hard_cite_class_cannot_soften:statistic/soften_to_illustration — must be supported, weak_anchor, or needs_allowlist_expansion
claim[7]:hard_cite_class_cannot_soften:statistic/soften_to_illustration — must be supported, weak_anchor, or needs_allowlist_expansion
